Q1: I usually organized my study routine around listening to lectures and discussions, since I learn best by hearing things. I'd set aside blocks of time throughout the week, maybe 16 hours total, where I'd put on my headphones and really focus on audio content. With limited resources at home, I tried to maximize my time on campus or wherever I had good internet to download what I needed. I kept things flexible but consistent enough that I didn't fall too far behind.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due and what else was going on in my life. I tried to study at regular times, but honestly, deadlines would shift my priorities around. Some weeks I'd be super consistent, other weeks everything would bunch up toward the end. I found that having a loose structure helped me stay on track without stressing myself out too much.
Q3: Usually I'd log in when I knew there was new lecture content posted or when I needed to check on discussion threads. I'm pretty active in the discussions, so I'd often pop in just to see what my classmates were saying. Sometimes I'd log in out of habit, just scrolling through to make sure I wasn't missing anything important. Notifications on my phone would also prompt me to check in.
Q4: Yeah, checking the LMS became pretty automatic for me, especially the discussion boards. I genuinely enjoy the peer interactions, so logging in to see responses became part of my daily routine. It happened naturally because I found value in those conversations. Over time, it just felt like checking social media but for school stuff.
Q5: The video lectures were incredibly useful for me because I'm an auditory learner. I'd watch them multiple times, really absorbing the explanations. The discussion forums were also great because hearing different perspectives helped concepts click. I spent a lot of time on those videos, probably close to 80 minutes worth, because that's where I learned best.
Q6: I'll be honest, I didn't complete a lot of the written assignments on time, even though I knew they mattered. Some of the reading materials I'd skim or skip because they weren't as engaging as listening to lectures. I think when resources are text-heavy and I can't hear someone explaining it, I lose interest faster. It's not that they weren't valuable, they just didn't match how I learn.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could actually hear someone explaining concepts or when I could engage with peers about ideas. If it sparked some kind of discussion or made me think differently, I was all in. Honestly, if something felt repetitive or like busy work, I'd get bored quickly. I need that interactive or auditory element to stay engaged.
Q8: Optional activities felt more like opportunities than obligations, which made them more appealing in some ways. Required ones sometimes felt like checkboxes, which could get tedious. But I'd often engage more deeply with optional discussions because there was no pressure. The required stuff I'd do, but sometimes just to get it done rather than to really learn.
Q9: Absolutely, I did a lot of studying that wasn't visible in the LMS. I'd download lecture audio and listen while commuting or doing chores at home. With limited resources, I had to be strategic about when I used data, so offline studying was essential. I'd also talk through concepts with friends or family, which helped me process things audibly.
Q10: There were definitely times I was deeply engaged but the LMS wouldn't show it. Like when I'd listen to a downloaded lecture three times while walking around, that's not logged anywhere. Or when I'd discuss course concepts with classmates over the phone instead of in the forums. My learning happened a lot outside the digital space.
Q11: All the time. I'd access a video once, download the audio if I could, and then replay it offline multiple times. That single login doesn't capture the hours I spent reviewing that content. It was a necessity given my internet situation, but it also just worked better for my learning style.
Q12: There were moments when the material felt repetitive or when I already understood the concept, and I'd just feel bored. When that happened, I'd disengage a bit and not put as much effort into assignments. It wasn't stress or anxiety, just a lack of stimulation. The mindfulness exercises helped me refocus when I noticed myself drifting.
Q13: Competing responsibilities definitely affected my LMS use. I'd have to prioritize what I could do with limited time and resources. Fatigue would hit sometimes, and I'd just listen passively rather than actively engaging. But I managed my stress pretty well, so it never became overwhelming.
Q14: Yeah, sometimes I'd have the LMS open but I'd be zoning out, not really absorbing anything. I'd watch a video but my mind would wander because I was bored with the content. Those moments happened more when the material wasn't challenging or when it was purely text-based. I was present but not really engaged.
Q15: I definitely postponed assignments even when I knew they were important. Part of it was boredom, part of it was that I prioritized the learning activities I enjoyed more. I'd tell myself I'd get to the written work later, but sometimes later never came. My assignment completion rate reflects that struggle.
Q16: Deadlines would create these bursts of activity for me. I'd suddenly be very focused and productive right before something was due. Otherwise, I'd pace myself more casually. It wasn't ideal, but the pressure helped me prioritize when I needed to.
Q17: Feedback motivated me when it was constructive and specific. If I got good feedback on a discussion post, I'd be more likely to engage in future discussions. But if feedback was delayed or generic, it didn't really push me to change anything. Immediate responses from peers were actually more motivating than formal instructor feedback.
Q18: The course structure gave me flexibility, which I appreciated, but maybe too much flexibility for my own good. Having so many online courses meant I had to self-manage a lot, and sometimes things slipped through the cracks. The discussion-based elements kept me coming back though. A more structured approach to deadlines might have helped my completion rates.
Q19: The video content and discussion forums made engagement easier because they matched my learning style. What made it harder was when there was too much reading or when assignments felt disconnected from the discussions. Also, not having reliable internet access at home created barriers that the course design didn't account for.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is genuinely trying to learn and explore but struggles with the formal assessment part. Maybe they're engaging with content, participating in discussions, but the assignments don't click for them. Or they could be dealing with external pressures that make it hard to produce work even when they're actively learning.
Q21: That definitely reflects some of my experience. A lot of my real learning happened offline, listening to downloaded content or discussing with peers outside the platform. Someone could perform well because they're efficiently using materials outside the LMS. Low activity doesn't mean low engagement, just different engagement.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of when you're online and what you click on, which gives some insight into habits. But they miss the quality of engagement, the offline studying, and the informal learning that happens in conversations. They definitely can't capture whether you're actually absorbing information or just going through the motions.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm actively thinking about the material, connecting it to other ideas, and wanting to discuss it with others. It's when I'm curious and not just checking boxes. For me, engagement is about mental involvement, not just physical presence in the system. It's when learning feels alive rather than like a chore.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I learned through listening and re-listening to content offline. It wouldn't capture my conversations with classmates outside the platform or how I processed ideas while doing other activities. My resilience and ability to manage stress also wouldn't be visible, but those definitely influenced how I approached the course. The data shows activity, not the depth of my learning.
